questions.question_text: Who was the founder of the Vijayanagara empire?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara Empire, also called the Karnata Kingdom, was a Hindu empire based in the region of South India. It was established in 1336 by the brothers Harihara I and Bukka Raya I of the Sangama dynasty with the help of Guru Madhava Vidyaranya. It lasted until 1646, although its power declined after a major military defeat in the Battle of Talikota in 1565 by the combined armies of the Deccan sultanates. The empire is named after its capital city of Vijayanagara, whose ruins surround present-day Hampi, now a World Heritage Site in Karnataka, India. Four dynasties ruled over Vijayanagar: Sangama Dynasty (1336-1485), Saluva Dynasty (1485-1505), Tuluva Dynasty (1505-1570), and Aravidu Dynasty (till middle of 17th century).

questions.question_text: Which famous war took place in 1565 AD?

options.E: None of the above/More one of the above

questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota was fought in January 1565 in the battlefield of Rakshas and Tagri — the two villages against Vijaynagar. In this battle, Aliya Rama Raya of Vijay Nagar was defeated and executed by the joint army of Deccan states. This battle diminished the supremacy of the Vijaynagar Empire. After this battle, the capital of Vijay Nagar was shifted to Penugonda and then Chandragiri.

questions.question_text: Who of the following founded a new city on the south bank of a tributary to river Krishna and undertook to rule his new kingdom as the agent of a deity to whom all the land south of the river Krishna was supposed to belong?
questions.explanation: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I founded the Vijayanagar Empire which was situated on the bank of Tungabhadra River (southern tributary of river Krishna) which gradually expanded to the regions of Konkon and Malabar. Amoghavarsha I ruled the Rashtrakuta kingdom, Ballala II ruled the Hoysala Kingdom, and Prataparudra II ruled the Kakatiya Kingdom.

questions.question_text: Who among the following famous rulers founded a suburban township of Nagalapuram, near Vijayanagar, after his mother's name?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Krishnadevaraya founded a suburban township near Vijayanagar called Nagalapuram in memory of his mother Nagamamba Devi. Presently it lies in the Tirupati district of Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question_text: Krishnadevaraya of the Vijayanagar Empire was a contemporary of
questions.explanation: Babur (14 February 1483 – 26 December 1530), a descendant of Timur and Genghis Khan, founded the Mughal Empire. Babur recognised Rana Sanga's skill in leadership, calling him one of the two greatest non-Muslim Indian kings of the time, the other being Krishnadevaraya of Vijayanagara. Krishnadeva Raya (17 January 1471 – 17 October 1529) was the most influential ruler of the Tuluva dynasty. Babur mentions him in his memoir 'Tuzuk-i-Baburi'.

questions.question_text: Which Arab Traveller widely described the importance of the Rashtrakutas?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Abu Sulaiman was an Arab traveller who visited the capital of Rashtrakutas in 851 CE. According to him, among four of the great empires of the world, the Rashtrakuta empire is one of them. Al-Masudi (also known as Arab Herodotus) gave an extensive account of India in his work 'Muruj-ul-Zahab'. Al-Biruni was brought by Mahmud Ghaznavi and compiled 'Tehqiq-i-Hind' or Kitab-ul-Hind. Ibn Batuta was from Morocco and wrote 'Rihala'.

questions.question_text: Vatsraja Was the Famous King of Which Dynasty?

options.E: None Of the Above/More Than One of The Above

questions.explanation: Vatsaraja (780–800) or Vatsraja was a prominent ruler of Northern India's Pratihara dynasty and was also Nagabhata I's grand-nephew. Mihirbhoja was the most prominent ruler of the Pratihara Dynasty. The capital of Pratiharas was Kannauj. An important dramatist during Gurjara-Pratihara was 'Rajshekhara' who wrote the famous 'Karpuramanjari' and Kavya Mimansa.

questions.question_text: Who was the founder of the Karnata dynasty?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Karnat dynasty was founded by Nanyadev in the Mithila region of Bihar in 1097 AD. Capital: Simraon (Simraongarh, Champaran). Bakhtiar Khalji attacked during 1197-98 in the reign of Narsimhadeva. Harisimha also known as Harisimhadeva was the last ruler of this dynasty. When Gyasuddin Tughlaq attacked the Mithila region, Harisimha lost the war and fled to Nepal.

questions.question_text: Who was the last King of the Karnata dynasty?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Harisimha (also known as Harisimhadeva) was the last ruler of the Karnata dynasty. When Gyasuddin Tughlaq attacked the Mithila region, a war was fought between the two where Harisimha lost the war and fled to Nepal. He promoted the culture of Bihar and started 'Taleju (Goddess)' worship in Nepal.

questions.question_text: The Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur was constructed by which King?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Brihadishvara Temple, dedicated to Lord Shiva, is located on the banks of the Kaveri River. It is an example of Dravidian architecture which flourished roughly from the 8th century to about the 13-14 century AD. It was built by emperor Rajaraja Chola I between 1003 AD and 1010 AD. It is also called Dakshina Meru (Meru of the South). The temple is a part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site known as the 'Great Living Chola Temples', along with the Gangaikonda Cholapuram temple and Airavatesvara temple.

questions.question_text: With reference to the art and archaeological history of India, which one among the following was made earliest?
questions.explanation: The Rock-cut Elephant at Dhauli (located in the ancient territory of Kalinga) is the earliest Buddhist sculpture of Odisha, constructed by Ashoka in 272-231 BC, which carries a sculpted elephant facing east. The Lingaraj Temple was built in the 11th century AD. The rock-cut monuments at Mahabalipuram were carved in the 7th and 8th centuries by the Pallava kings. The Udayagiri Caves near Vidisha were made by the Guptas during the early 5th century CE.

questions.question_text: Muhammad Ghori was defeated by the Chalukyas of Gujarat in

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Muhammad Ghori captured Multan in 1175 AD and tried to invade India in 1178 AD. The region of Gujarat and Rajasthan at that time was under the Chalukyan ruler. Mularaja-II or Bhima-II, under the guidance of his capable widowed mother Naayika Devi, valiantly fought back and successfully defeated Ghori near Mount Abu in the Battle of Kayadara or Kasahrada (1178 AD). This was the first defeat of Ghori in India. In 1191 AD, Prithviraj Chauhan defeated Ghori in the first battle of Tarain. In 1192 AD, Muhammad Ghori defeated Prithviraj Chauhan in the second battle of Tarain.

questions.question_text: The famous Meenakshi temple in Madurai was built during which Dynasty?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Identify the cartographer who was credited for creating Tabula Rogeriana, one of the most advanced medieval world maps.

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Marco Polo said about this temple that this temple is 'the brightest star in the galaxy of medieval temples of the Deccan'. Which temple is talked about?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Which medieval ruler is known for performing the ritual of hiranya garbha?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Which sultan of the Delhi Sultanate was known as 'Lakh Baksh'?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Qutub-ud-din-Aibak was born in Central Asia to Turkic parents and eventually became a slave of Muhammad of Ghori. After Ghori's assassination in 1206, Aibak became the de facto ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. He was known as a generous king and therefore was called Lakhbaksh. He is most remembered for laying the foundation of the Qutb Minar in Delhi, completed by Iltutmish. He also constructed Qawat-ul-Islam at Delhi and the mosque 'Adhai din ka Jhopra' in Ajmer, Rajasthan. He died in 1210 while playing a game similar to Polo called 'chovgan'.

questions.question_text: What is 'Dhai Din Ka Jhonpra'?
questions.explanation: Adhai Din Ka Jhopra is a mosque built by Qutub-ud-din Aibak in Ajmer, Rajasthan.

questions.question_text: Who was the first Indian Muslim to become the Sultan of Delhi?

options.E: None/more than one

questions.explanation: Nasiruddin Khusrau Shah was the only converted Indian Muslim to become the head of the Delhi Sultanate. He was a general in Alauddin Khalji's army and murdered Alauddin's successor Mubarak Shah Khalji to briefly become the Sultan of Delhi.

questions.question_text: The Muslim ruler whose empire was regarded as a part of Dar-ul-Islam was,

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Iltutmish was considered the 'Real Founder of Delhi Sultanate.' He made Delhi the capital in place of Lahore, introduced Silver Coin (tanka) and Copper coin (jital), organised the Iqta System, and set up an official nobility of slaves known as Chahalghani/Chalisa (Group of 40). He completed the construction of Qutub Minar. His empire was considered part of Dar ul-Islam meaning 'house/abode of Islam'.

questions.question_text: The Qutub Minar was completed by?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The founder of the Slave dynasty, Qutub-ud-din Aibak started the construction of the Qutub Minar in memory of the famous Sufi saint Khawaja Qutubuddin Bhaktiyar Kaki. But he could complete only the first storey of the building. The Qutub Minar was later completed by Iltutmish. The Hauz Shamsi, south of the Qutub Minar, and the madrasah around it, was also built by Iltutmish.

questions.question_text: Who established the Madrasa in Delhi?
questions.explanation: Iltutmish was the first ruler of Delhi Sultanate to establish madrasas in Delhi. He built a madrasa around Hauz Shamsi in Delhi. During this period, two important madrasas were founded: the Mu'zziyya named after Muhammad Ghuri and founded by Sultan Iltutmish, and the Naṣiriyya, named after Naṣir al-Din Maḥmud and built by Balban.

questions.question_text: The 'Iqtadari' system was introduced by
questions.explanation: The Iqtadari system was a system of administration during the Medieval period. The first ruler reported to have assigned 'small iqtas' to the soldiers in lieu of salaries was Iltutmish. The Delhi Sultanate's territories were divided into Iqtas given to Sultan's trusted officials, nobles, and warriors for administration. These land or iqta holders were called Iqtadar or Muqti or Wali. The muqtis led military campaigns, maintained law and order in their iqtas, and collected tax as salary.

questions.question_text: Who among the following opposed the power of the Khalifa?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Both Alauddin Khilji and Muhammad Bin Tughlaq were opposed to the power of Khalifa and ulema. Alauddin Khalji was the first king of Delhi who established the control of the state over religion. He was the most powerful Sultan of the Delhi Sultanate and had the desire to become the Second Alexander. He put the concepts of 'Paimaish' and 'Biswa' as measurements of cultivable land, demanded 50% of the cultivated crop as revenue, set up three markets at Delhi, and introduced the system of Dagh (branding of horses) and Chehra (descriptive role of soldiers). He built Alai Darwaza in 1311.

questions.question_text: Who started the Public Distribution System in the Sultanate period?

options.E: None of the above/More than one

questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji was the first ruler to introduce the Public Distribution System in the Sultanate period. His market reforms aimed to maintain a large and efficient army for keeping the Mongols in check. He established Diwan-i-Riyasat under an officer called Naib-i-Riyasat, fixed prices of various commodities, and set up three markets at Delhi for foodgrains, cloth, and horses/slaves/cattle.

questions.question_text: 'Thus, to Ala-ud-din belongs the credit of being the first Turkish Sultan of Delhi to bring the church under the control of the state and to usher in factors that might make the state in theory.' Who wrote these words?

options.E: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Ala-ud-din was the first Sultan of Delhi who did not pursue the principles of Islam, did not feel the necessity of seeking guidance from the Ulema, and did not even desire to use the name of Khalifa in matters concerning the state. It is in this context that Dr. A.L. Srivastava writes in his book 'Dilli Sultanate': 'Thus to Ala-ud-din belongs the credit of being the first Turkish Sultan of Delhi to bring the church under the control of the state and to usher in factors that might make the state secular in theory.'

questions.question_text: Which Sultan demanded half the crops on land after measurement as revenue?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji demanded 50% of the cultivated crop as revenue after thorough measurement. He put the concepts of 'Paimaish' and 'Biswa' as measurements of the cultivable land. According to the measurement system, the state demanded revenue based on standard production.

questions.question_text: Which Sultan Introduced Dagh and Chehra in The Army?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji introduced the system of Dagh (branding of horses) and Chehra (descriptive roll of soldiers) as part of his army reforms to maintain a large and efficient army.

questions.question_text: The system of 'Dagh' and 'Huliya' was introduced by
questions.explanation: Ala-ud-din Khilji introduced the system of Dagh (branding of horses) and Huliya/Chehra (descriptive roll/identity card of soldiers) as part of his military reforms.

questions.question_text: Which Sultan is famous for market control?
questions.explanation: Alauddin Khilji introduced detailed market reforms (Zawabit). He created Diwan-i-Riyasat under Naib-i-Riyasat. He set up three markets at Delhi: the first for foodgrains, the second for cloth and expensive items, and the third for horses, slaves and cattle. Each market was put under a Shuhna or controller. He also appointed Diwan-i-Riyasat and Shahna-i-Mandi to check the market, and received daily reports from Shuhna, barids (intelligence officers) and munshis (secret spies).

questions.question_text: Which Delhi Sultan is known for adopting the 'blood and iron' policy?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Ghiyas ud din Balban (1266-1286) was the slave of Iltutmish and belonged to the Mamluk dynasty. He was known for the adoption of 'the blood and iron policy' — ruthless to enemies, use of sword, harshness and strictness. He declared himself as the Shadow of God, introduced Persian traditions (Sijda, Pabos, Jamnibas), introduced Naurauz (Persian new year), reorganized the war office as Diwan-e-Arz, and suppressed the 'Corps of Forty' (Turkan-e-Chahalgani).

questions.question_text: 'Diwan-E-Arj' department was associated with:

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Diwan-E-Arj, created by Balban, was associated with the Defence or military department. It was headed by the ariz-i mumalik.

questions.question_text: Who was the first Muslim ruler to formulate the 'theory of kingship' similar to the 'theory of the divine right of the kings'?

options.E: None of these/More than one of these

questions.explanation: Balban declared himself as the Shadow of God. His theory of kingship states that the king was the representative of God on the earth and Kingship was a divine institution. He declared this to make the nobles believe that he got the crown not through their mercy but by the mercy of God. He took the title 'zil-i-illahi' which means reflection of God.

questions.question_text: 'His theory of kingship was based on the principles of prestige, power, and justice.' Who said these words about Balban?

options.E: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Zia-ud-din Barani in his book 'Tarikh-i-Firuzshahi' argues that the Theory of Kingship was needed to create fear among the masses so that they obey the king, and this had to be done by showing glory and power to nobles and masses.

questions.question_text: Tughril Khan raised a standard of revolt during whose reign?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Tughril Khan aka Tughral Tughan Khan was an officer of the Sultanate of Delhi. Balban appointed him as the governor of Lakhnauti, but Tughril Khan proclaimed himself as the sultan. Both officials sent by Balban were defeated by Tughril Khan. Balban personally led a campaign against Tughril Khan in 1280, which lasted for two years until he finally succeeded in suppressing the rebellion.

questions.question_text: Which Delhi Sultan built the maximum number of canals?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Firoz Shah Tughlaq was the third ruler of the Tughlaq dynasty, ruling from 1351 to 1388. In order to encourage irrigation, he built the largest number of irrigation canals in the Sultanate period. He repaired canals and imposed Haque-i-Sharb or Hasil-i-Sharb (water tax). He also repaired the Qutub Minar, built Diwan-i-Khairat (office for charity), Diwan-i-Bundagan (department of slaves), founded Firozabad, Fatehabad, Jaunpur, and Hissar, and established hospitals known as Dar-ul-Shifa.

questions.question_text: Which Sultan of Delhi Sultanate established a charitable hospital named 'Dar-ul-Shifa'?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Firuz Shah Tughlaq established hospitals known as Dar-ul-Shifa (also called Bimaristan or Shifa Khana). He also built Diwan-i-Khairat (office for charity), Diwan-i-Bundagan (department of slaves), sarais (rest houses), and four new towns: Firozabad, Fatehabad, Jaunpur, and Hissar.

questions.question_text: Which ruler established a department for the Slaves (Gulam)?
questions.explanation: Firuz Shah Tughlaq established a separate department for slaves called Diwan-i-Bundagan (also called Diwan-i-Bandagan). The officer in charge of this department was Wakil-i-dar.

questions.question_text: Which city of India was called 'Shiraz-i-Mashriq' (Shiraz of the East) in the medieval age?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The city of Jaunpur was called the Shiraz-i-Mashriq or Shiraz of the East. It was built in 1359 by Sultan Firoz Shah Tughlaq on the river Gomti and was named after Sultan's cousin and predecessor, Jauna Khan. After Timur's invasion, Malik Sarwar, a Khawaja Sara (eunuch) appointed as governor of Jaunpur in 1394 with the title of Malik-us-Sharq (ruler of the east), declared independence and laid the foundation of the Sharqi kingdom.

questions.question_text: By Which Name, Firoz Shah Tughlaq Translated Sanskrit Manuscript into Persian?

options.E: None Of the Above/More Than One of The Above

questions.explanation: Firoz Shah Tughlaq plundered Nagarkot in Kangra in 1365 AD and gathered 1300 Sanskrit manuscripts from the library of the famous Jwalamukhi temple. He got them translated into Persian under the title Dalail-i-Piroz-Shahi by writer Arizuddin Khan. Firoz Shah Tughlaq is also known as the Father of Irrigation for constructing several gardens and canals.

questions.question_text: What was the force behind Firuz Shah Tughluq's policy of granting Jagirs?

options.E: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Jagirdar system, first developed during the Delhi Sultanate period, was a form of land tenancy. The system was abolished by Alauddin Khilji and was revived by Firoz Shah Tughlaq. He granted jagirs to his military officials in lieu of cash salaries due to the exigencies of the day, as the introduction of token currency by his predecessor Muhammad Bin Tughlaq had proved disastrous with open forgery of coins.

questions.question_text: The earliest description of Bakhtiyar khilji's invasion of Bihar is in

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Minhaj-i-Siraj, a Persian historian, composed Tabqat-I-Nasiri which gives a detailed account of the military achievements and invasion by Bakhtiyar Khilji. Bakhtiyar Khilji was the first Turk to invade Bihar. He destroyed Nalanda University, Odantapuri University, and Vikramshila University. Bakhtiyar Khilji was killed by his associate Alimardan and his tomb is in Imadpur, Bihar Sharif.

questions.question_text: Who was deputed for the conquest of Dwarasamudra by Ala-ud-din Khalji?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Malik Kafur was a prominent slave-general of Alauddin Khalji. He defeated the Mongol invaders in 1306 and led expeditions against the Yadavas (1308), the Kakatiyas (1310), the Hoysalas (1311), and the Pandyas (1311). In February 1311, Malik Kafur besieged the Hoysala capital Dwarasamudra, and the defending ruler Veera Ballala III surrendered and agreed to pay an annual tribute.

questions.question_text: Who was the last Hindu ruler of Delhi?
questions.explanation: Hemu (Hem Chandra Vikramaditya) rose to prominence under Sher Shah's army. He defeated the Mughal army led by Tardi Beg Khan at the Battle of Tughlaqabad on 7 October 1556 and occupied Delhi. He had his coronation at Purana Qila and assumed the name 'Vikramaditya' or Raja Bikramjit. Though he ruled only for 29 days, he was the last Hindu ruler of Delhi. He was captured and beheaded by Bairam Khan at the 2nd Battle of Panipat on November 5, 1556 AD.

questions.question_text: Which sultan of Delhi was a contemporary of the Mongol leader Genghis Khan?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Delhi Sultan who wrote his memoirs, was:

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: 'Rihla' has been written by?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Which of the following tax imposed during the reign of Firoz Shah Tughlaq?

options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Options A, B, and C were all imposed during the reign of Firoz Shah Tughlaq.

questions.question_text: Shaikh Bahauddin Zakaria belonged to which sect?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Shaikh Bahauddin Zakaria was the founder of the Suhrawardi Silsilah in India. It was founded by Shaikh Shihabuddin Suhrawardi, popular in Multan, Lahore, and Sindh. This silsilah is considered more orthodox than Chisti. The Suhrawardis accepted royal grants and took active part in politics. Baha-ud-din Zakaria was the greatest saint of this silsilah and was given the title of Shaikh-ul-Islam by Iltutmish.

questions.question_text: In which Sufi order was Sufi saint Sharfuddin Yahya Maneri associated?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Makhdoom Sharfuddin Ahmed bin Yahya Maneri was a 13th-century Sufi saint who belonged to the Firdausi Silsilah, born on 16 Sept 1262 at Maner, near Patna, Bihar. He was a disciple of Sheikh Najeebuddin Firdausi, spent 30 years in the forests of Rajgir and later settled in Bihar Sharif. The Firdausi silsila was a branch of the Suhrawardi order. Maneri is also known as Makhdum-e-Jahan and his letters to his disciple were clubbed as 'Maktubat-e-Sadi', translated into English by Paul Jackson.

questions.question_text: Nizamuddin Auliya was the follower of which Sufi saint?
questions.explanation: Nizamuddin Auliya (also known as Mahboob-e-ilahi) belonged to the Chishtiya Order, which was founded in India by Khwaja Moin-Uddin Chishti in 1192. The Chisti influence at Delhi was firmly established by Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki. Nizamuddin Auliya adapted yogic breathing practices and was named a Sidh by Yogis. His famous disciple was Amir Khusrow, called 'father of Qawwali' and 'Parrot of India'.

questions.question_text: The followers of Ramanuja are known as:

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Ramananda was the Guru of

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Dargah of which Sufi saint is in Ajmer?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Which of the following Sufi saint believed that devotional music was one way of moving closer to God?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Pietra Dura is related to which of the following?

options.A: Decorating the walls with floral designs made of semi-precious stones
options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Pietra dura was an element of decoration of walls with the use of semi-precious, highly polished colour stones to create images. This decorative technique was gaining popularity during the reign of Shah Jahan, whose period is known as the Golden Age of Mughal Architecture. Shah Jahan built praiseworthy buildings and forts like the Taj Mahal, The Red Fort, Jama Masjid, etc.

questions.question_text: Who was the first Mughal ruler who fought against the British?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Josiah Child was Governor of Surat and Bombay and a trade negotiation with Mughal King Aurangzeb (1689) broke off. This angered the officials of EIC and they waged a war against the Mughals. They were defeated and begged for pardon. Aurangzeb also imposed fines on them.

questions.question_text: Who wrote Khulasat-ut-Tawarikh?
questions.explanation: Khulasat-ut-Tawarikh is a Persian language chronicle written by Sujan Rai Khatri in 1695 CE during the reign of the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb. It deals with the history of Hindustan and contains details about the contemporary Mughal Empire such as people and their customs, flora and fauna, and the geography of subahs (provinces) of the Mughal Empire.

questions.question_text: Where is Sher Shah's tomb located?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Sher Shah's tomb is located in Sasaram. Sher Shah Suri, originally named Farid, was the son of Hasan Khan, the jagirdar of Sasaram, and was the founder of the Sur Empire in India. He introduced the Ryotwari system, standardized silver Rupiya, divided his empire into 47 Sarkars (each further divided into Parganas), and fought the Battle of Chausa (1539) and Battle of Kannauj (1540) defeating Humayun.

questions.question_text: Sher Shah's Land Revenue System Can Be Compared with Which of The Following Systems?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Sher Shah's revenue model can be compared with the Ryotwari system. Sher Shah introduced the Ryotwari system in most of the places during which the state kept direct relations with the peasants for the assessment and collection of land revenue. Pattas were issued to the peasants and Qabuliyats were received from them.

questions.question_text: Who among the following rulers 'divided his troops into units of two hundred, two hundred fifty and five hundred'?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: BPSC has chosen the E option though various sources cite Islam Shah as the answer. Islam Shah, the second son of Sher Shah, was the second ruler of the Suri dynasty. He was known for strengthening the army and artillery. He fixed regular grades in the service in the army and divided his troops into units of 200, 250, and 500. He also added to the defence of the Empire by building 5 more fortresses: Shergarh, Islamgarh, Rashidgarh, Firozgarh, and Mankot.

questions.question_text: Who became the virtual ruler of South Bihar in 1503?

options.E: None/more than one

questions.explanation: Sher Khan (later known as Sher Shah Suri) became the virtual ruler of South Bihar in 1503.

questions.question_text: 'No government, not even the British has shown so much wisdom as this Pathan.' Who wrote these words about Sher Shah Suri?

options.E: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Henry George Keene was an English historian of medieval and modern India. In his book 'The Turks in India; critical chapters on the Administration of that country by the Chughtai, Baìbar, and his descendants', he says about Sher Shah Suri: 'No Government-not even the British-has so much wisdom as this Pathan.' According to Erskine, 'Sher Shah had more of the spirit of a legislator and a guardian of his people than any prince before Akbar.' According to Dr Smith, 'If Sher Shah had been spared, the Great Mughals would not have appeared on the stage of history.'

questions.question_text: Where was the battle between Sher Shah and Humayun fought on 26 June 1539?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Battle of Chausa was fought between Sher Shah and Humayun on 26 June 1539 near Buxar, in which Humayun was defeated. Humayun's life was saved by a person called 'Nizam'.

questions.question_text: Who among the following made an offer to Humayun that he would surrender Bihar and pay an annual tribute of 10 lakh dinars if he was allowed to retain Bengal?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Sher Khan fortified his position and became the dominant ruler of Bihar. Sher Khan captured the powerful fort of Rohtas and invaded Bengal, capturing Gaurs. After triumphing over Gaurs, Sher Shah made an offer to Humayun that he would surrender Bihar and pay an annual tribute of 10 lakh Dinars if Sher Shah was allowed to retain Bengal. Humayun rejected this offer. Eventually Sher Shah defeated Humayun at the Battle of Chausa, near Buxar (c. 1539 CE), and adopted the title of Sher Shah Sultan-e-Adil.

questions.question_text: Where is Humayun's Tomb situated?
questions.explanation: Humayun died in 1556 while climbing down the stairs of the library (at Din Panah). His widow Hamida Banu Begam (Haji Begam) commenced the construction of his tomb in 1569, fourteen years after his death. Mirak Mirza Ghiyath, a Persian, was the architect. Humayun's Tomb is created out of red sandstone, in the charbagh style. It is also called the 'dormitory of the Mughals' as it contains over 150 Mughal family members. The tomb was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993. Humayun's sister, Gulbadan Begum, wrote his biography Humayunama.

questions.question_text: During the time of which Mughal emperor Sir Thomas Roe came to India?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: In 1615, Sir Thomas Roe, an ambassador of King James I of England, came to the court of Jahangir. Initially Jahangir was resistant; later he granted permission to the English to establish a trading port at Surat. He stayed in the court of Jahangir for three years till 1618. Earlier, William Hawkins visited Mughal Emperor Jahangir in 1608 but could not secure a permit to establish a factory at Surat.

questions.question_text: English traveller William Hawkins visited India during the time of which Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: William Hawkins was a representative of the English East India Company and the commander of Hector. He was the first Englishman to visit the court of Jahangir in 1608 (as he was well versed in the Turkish language). He was not given permission to establish a trading port at Surat. He stayed in his court for nearly three years and developed a great bonding with Jahangir, who referred to him as 'English Khan'.

questions.question_text: Mughal painting reached its Zenith under

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Jahangir had a deep interest in painting art and under him, Mughal painting achieved its zenith. During Jahangir, the Persian and Indian style of painting was fully synthesised. He was a keen naturalist with preference for paintings of hunting scenes, birds, and flowers. He continued the tradition of portraiture. Abul Hasan, Mansur, Bishan Das, Goverdhan, Balchand, Daulat, Mukhlis, Bhim, and Inayat were famous painters in his court.

questions.question_text: Which Mughal Emperor hung a bell of justice called Janji-i-Adli?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Jahangir installed a bell of justice called Janjir-i-adil hung outside his palace in Agra in 1605 right after he became the King. It was hung so that those aggrieved by the administration of justice might come to the chain and shake it, and the emperor might hear it. He also laid several gardens such as the Shalimar and Nishat gardens in Kashmir, and built the Moti Masjid in Lahore.

questions.question_text: Which Mughal Ruler Hung a Chain and Bell Called Zanjir-I-Adil for Justice?

options.E: None of the Above/More Than One of The Above

questions.explanation: Jahangir installed the Zanjir-i-Adil (Chain of Justice) hung outside his palace in Agra in 1605 so that aggrieved persons could directly appeal to the emperor.

questions.question_text: Who among the following Mughal Emperors shifted emphasis from illustrated manuscripts to the album and individual portraits?
questions.explanation: During Jahangir's reign there was a shift of emphasis in Mughal painting from illustrated manuscripts to the album and individual portraits. Under his patronage, Mughal painting achieved naturalism and scientific accuracy. Individual Mughal paintings known as 'The Muraqqas' became popular under Jahangir's patronage. Famous painters included Aqa Riza and his son Abul Hasan (given the title 'Nadir-ul-Zaman' by Jahangir), Ustad Mansur (great naturalist), Faruq Begh, Muhammad Nadir, Madhav, Bishan Das, Manohar, Goverdhan, Balchand, Daulat, Mukhlis, Bhim and Inayat.

questions.question_text: From among the great Mughal rulers, Babur to Aurangzeb two were born of 'Rajput Princess'. They were

options.E: None/more than one

questions.explanation: Jahangir was born to Rajput Princess 'Jodhabai'. Her name in Mughal chronicles was Mariam-uz-Zamani. This title was given to her by her husband Akbar after she gave birth to Jahangir. Shah Jahan's biological mother was Jagat Gosaini (known to the Mughals as Bilqis Makani), a Rajput princess from Marwar.

questions.question_text: Ain-I-Akbari was written by

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Ain-I-Akbari was written by Abul Fazl, a close friend and courtier of Akbar. Abul Fazl wrote a three-volume Book on Akbar's Reign named 'Akbar Nama'. The 1st volume contains details about the ancestors of Akbar, the 2nd provides chronological details of Akbar's reign, and the 3rd (Ain-i-Akbari) deals with Akbar's administration, household army, revenue system, and geographical extent of his Empire. The Akbar Nama was translated into English by Henry Beveridge.

questions.question_text: Which Mughal ruler established Karkhanas for Paintings?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Akbar established Karkhanas or Tasvir Khanas for painting during his reign. The main themes in Mughal Paintings were court scenes, battles, hunting scenes, and portraits. During the reign of Akbar, paintings were based on tales and epics of Mahabharata, Ramayana, and naturalism. Famous painters of his time were Dashwant, Baswan, and Kesu.

questions.question_text: Din-i-ilahi in 1582 AD was promulgated by
questions.explanation: The Din-i-Ilahi known during its time as Tawḥid-i-Ilahi ('Divine Monotheism') or Divine Faith was propounded by the Mughal emperor Akbar in 1582 AD. Din-i-Ilahi comprises many existing religions including Hinduism, Christianity, Zoroastrianism. There were no rituals or beliefs, nor any book or priesthood. 'Sulhi-Kul' policy of Akbar reflects his religious tolerance. Birbal was the first Hindu to join Din-i-Ilahi.

questions.question_text: What was the name of the Persian translation of Mahabharata?
questions.explanation: The Persian translation of the Sanskrit epic Mahabharata is called Razmnama, commissioned by Akbar, translated by Faizi and Abd al-Qadir Badauni. It was a product of the Maktab Khana (translation house) introduced by Akbar in 1574. In 1587, Abul Fazl was appointed to compose a preface to the text. Abdul Quadir Badauni also translated Ramayana into Persian. The Harivamsa was translated into Persian by Maulana Shiri.

questions.question_text: With reference to Mian Tansen, which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.A: Tansen was the title given to him by emperor Akbar.
options.B: Tansen composed dhrupads on Hindu gods and goddesses.
options.C: Tansen composed songs for his patrons.

questions.explanation: Tansen began his career in the court and patronage of the Hindu king of Rewa, Raja Ramchandra Singh, where Tansen's original name was Ramtanu. Akbar considered him as one of the Navaratnas and gave him the title 'Mian', an honorific meaning learned man — not 'Tansen'. Tansen is remembered for his epic Dhrupad compositions, creating several new ragas, and writing two classic books on music: Sri Ganesh Stotra and Sangita Sara.

questions.question_text: 'After uniting India, he wanted to unite the Mid-East and Western Asia with him as a very important step towards the establishment of a world empire.' R.P. Tripathi wrote about which ruler of Mughal India?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: R.P Tripathi in his book 'The Rise and Fall of the Mughal Empire' says about Akbar that after uniting India, he wanted to unite the Mid-East and Western Asia with him as a very important step towards the establishment of a world empire.

questions.question_text: In Medieval India, why was the Mansabdari system introduced?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Mansabdari system can be traced back to Changez Khan. Babur was the first to introduce it to North India. However, it was formally introduced by the Mughal emperor Akbar in 1571 AD. Mansabdar means a rank holder or an officer. Akbar attempted to integrate the nobility, armed forces, and bureaucracy into a common pool of administration. There was dual representation of mansab: Zat (personal rank and pay status) and Sawar (number of horsemen to maintain). The Mansab (office/rank of dignity) was not hereditary.

questions.question_text: With reference to medieval India, which one of the following is the correct sequence in ascending order in terms of size?
questions.explanation: The Mughal administrative hierarchy in ascending order: Gram (village) < Pargana (group of villages) < Sarkar (group of parganas) < Suba (provinces). The Muqaddam was the head of the gram. The shiqdar was the police chief assigned to Pargana. The sarkar had shiqdar-i-shiqdran and Faujdar (administration), Amalguzar (revenue), Kotwal (law and order) during the Mughal period. The Suba was headed by Sipahsalar, Diwan, and Bakshi.

questions.question_text: Ibadat Khana at Fatehpur Sikri was

options.A: The Mosque for The Use of Royal Family
options.C: The Hall in Which Akbar Held Discussions with Scholars of Various Religions.
options.D: The Room in Which the Nobles Belonging to Different Religions Gathered to Discuss Religious Affairs

questions.explanation: The Ibadat Khana, meaning the House of Worship, was a meeting house built in 1575 CE by the Mughal Emperor Akbar at Fatehpur Sikri to gather spiritual leaders of different religions for discussions on religious teachings. He invited mystics, intellectuals and theologians from Hinduism, Islam, Zoroastrianism, Christianity, and even atheists on Thursday evenings. In 1582, he discontinued the debates in the Ibadat Khana as it led to bitterness among different religions.

questions.question_text: European paintings were introduced in the court of

options.E: None of these/More than one of these

questions.explanation: European paintings were introduced at the court by Portuguese priests under the reign of Akbar. Under their influence, the principles of foreshortening were quickly adopted. Mughal painting developed under the patronage of the ruling Mughal emperors with its rise to prominence during the reign of Akbar in 1560 AD under the supervision of two Persian masters, Mir Sayyed Ali and Abdul Samad Khan. The two most celebrated works during Akbar were Tuti-Nama and Hamza Nama.

questions.question_text: 'Jaswant and Dasawan' famous painters were courtiers of which Mughal emperor:

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Jaswant and Dasawan were the painters in Akbar's court who painted Razim Namah, the Persian Mahabharata. Painting in its nascent stage was started in the Mughal period by Humayun, and the famous painters were Mir Saiyad Ali and Abbusamad. Akbar got a number of texts painted, beginning with 'Dastan-e-Amir Hamza'. Jehangir's reign was called the 'Golden period of painting'. Aurangzeb banned artwork and painting in the Court.

questions.question_text: The famous Buland Darwaza at Fatehpur Sikri forms which entrance to the Jama Masjid?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Buland Darwaza (Door of victory), a grand gateway constructed by Akbar in 1602 AD, was built to mark Akbar's triumph over Gujarat. Crafted from red sandstone and adorned with white marble, it is located in Fatehpur Sikri. It is a 15-storeyed gateway and the highest gateway in the world. This historic gateway serves as the southern entrance to Jama Masjid and is part of the Fatehpur Sikri complex (UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986).

questions.question_text: In Which Rajput's Palace, Emperor Akbar Was Born?

options.E: None of the above/more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Mughal emperor Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar was born in Amarkot, Pakistan in the palace of King Virsal on October 15, 1542 AD, when his father Humayun fled from military defeat at the hands of Sher Shah Suri. Amarkot or Umerkot was founded and ruled by Sodha Rajputs of the Parmar Dynasty, named after its founder Rana Amar Singh Sodha.

questions.question_text: In which language was 'Tuzuk-i-Baburi' written?
questions.explanation: Tuzuk-i-Baburi (also known as Baburnama) is the autobiography of Babur, the founder of the Mughal empire in India. It is written in the Chagatai language, an extinct Turkish literary language. The book records events of Babur's life from the age of 12 until he died in 1530, covering topics such as astronomy, geography, statecraft, military matters, plants and animals, poetry, music, paintings, and human nature. Baburnama was later translated into Persian during the reign of Akbar by Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khanan.

questions.question_text: Baburnama was composed in which language?
questions.explanation: Baburnama was composed in the Chagatai language, an extinct Turkish literary language. It was the autobiography of Babur, the founder of the Mughal empire. It was later translated into Persian during the reign of Akbar by Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khanan.

questions.question_text: 'Yoga Vasistha' was translated into Persian by Nizamuddin Panipati during the reign of:
questions.explanation: For the translation of Indian texts into Persian, Akbar established the Maktab Khana (a house of translation) in Fatehpur Sikri in 1574. Some important translations include the Mahabharata into the Razmnama and the Yoga Vasistha, which was translated by Nizamuddin Panipati into Persian, called Jug-Basisht/Yoga-Vasistha, during the reign of Akbar. Mahabharat was translated by Abul Fazl, Faizi and Abdul Quadri Badauni. Ramayana was translated by Abdul Quadri Badauni.

questions.question_text: How many parts are there in Akbarnama?
questions.explanation: The Akbarnama, also known as the Book of Akbar, is the official chronicle of the reign of Akbar, written by Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak. The Akbar Nama is divided into three books: The first book dealt with the birth of Akbar and the history of Timur's family, the reigns of Babur and Humayun, and the Suri sultans of Delhi. The second volume provides a comprehensive account of the history of Akbar's reign up to 1602. The third is the Ain-i Akbari, dealing with Akbar's administration, household, army, revenues, and geography of his empire. The Akbar Nama was translated into English in the early twentieth century by Henry Beveridge.

questions.question_text: Tabaqat-i-Akbari, which has been sometimes considered more reliable than Abul Fazl's Akbarnama was written by

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: The Tabaqat-I-Akbari is a three-volume chronicle compiled by Khwajah Nizamuddin Ahmed (appointed as a Bakshi by Emperor Akbar). The account covers the earliest Muslim invasions (from 986 AD) and concludes in the thirty-eighth year of Akbar's reign (1593-94 AD). The work has been translated into English by Dr Brajendranath De. Many historians like Elliot, Erskine, Lees, Wolseley Haig, and Beni Prasad regarded Tabaqat-i-Akbari as the most reliable source of information about Medieval India.

questions.question_text: Consider the following Bhakti Saints: 1. Dadu Dayal 2. Guru Nanak 3. Tyagaraja. Who among the above was/were preaching when the Lodi dynasty fell and Babur took over?
questions.explanation: Babur laid the foundation of the Mughal Empire after winning the First Battle of Panipat against Ibrahim Lodi on April 21, 1526. Guru Nanak (1469-1539) was the contemporary of Ibrahim Lodi and Babur. Dadu Dayal (1544-1603) was born after this event and was a contemporary of Akbar. Thyagaraja (1767-1847) lived much later. Therefore, only Guru Nanak was preaching when the Lodi dynasty fell and Babur took over.

questions.question_text: Who was the commander of Rana Pratap's army in the Battle of Haldighati?
questions.explanation: The commander of Rana Pratap's army in the Battle of Haldighati was Hakim Khan. The Battle of Haldighati was fought on 18 June 1576 between the armies of the Rana of Mewar, Maharana Pratap, and the Mughal emperor Akbar.

questions.question_text: In which of the following battles are the main opponents not correctly stated?

options.A: Second Battle of Panipat - Tipu Sultan and Marathas
options.B: Battle of Haldighati - Maharana Pratap and Akbar
options.C: First Battle of Panipat - Babur and Ibrahim Lodi
options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Panipat was fought between Hemu (Hem Chandra Vikramaditya) and the army of Akbar (under Bairam Khan) on November 5, 1556 — not between Tipu Sultan and Marathas. The Battle of Haldighati (18 June 1576) was between Maharana Pratap and Akbar's forces led by Man Singh I of Amber. The First Battle of Panipat was fought between Babur and Ibrahim Lodhi on April 21, 1526.

questions.question_text: During the Delhi Sultanate, the designation 'Muqaddam or Chaudhari' was used for

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.explanation: Muqaddam or Chaudhari was a term that referred to a village headman during the sultanate period. The state was divided into various administrative units: Iqta (province) headed by Muqtis or walis; Shiq (District) by Shiqdar; Paragana (Sub district) by Amil; and Village by Chaudhari or Muqaddam or khut.

questions.question_text: Jahangiri Mahal is related to which fort?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Tarikh-i-Shershahi was written by which of the following authors?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Who was the first great sultan to visit Muinuddin Chisti Dargah?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: 'Alamgirnama' was written by?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Who established Ashta Pradhan?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Ashta Pradhan literally means a council of ministers. It was a system of ministerial delegation in the Maratha empire. Chhatrapati Shivaji formalized the institution of a council of eight ministers on the occasion of his coronation in 1674 at the fort of Raigad. The council included: Peshwa (Finance and general administration), Majumdar/Amatya (Revenue and Finance minister), Waqenavis/Mantri (Home Minister), Dabir/Sumant (Foreign Minister), Surnavis/Sachiv (Head of Royal correspondence), Pandit Rao/Sadar (Head of religious affairs), Sar-i-Naubat/Senapati (Military commander), and Nyayadhish (Justice).

questions.question_text: Where was the capital of Shivaji?

options.E: None of the above/more than above

questions.explanation: Raigarh was the capital of the independent Maratha Kingdom established by Shivaji. Great Maratha ruler Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj was born on 19 February 1630 at Shivneri Fort in Pune District.

questions.question_text: Which Sultan was reputed to be the 'Akbar of Kashmir'?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Sultan Zainul Abidin was the eighth Sultan of Kashmir, known by his subjects as Bod Shah (Great King). He reigned for more than 50 years, had a liberal religious policy, promoted literature, art, and architecture, earning him the nickname 'Akbar of Kashmir'. He also abolished Jaziya for the Hindu minority of Kashmir and patronized Sanskrit literature.

questions.question_text: Paper came to be used in India in the

options.E: None of these/More than one of these

questions.explanation: As per the records of Al-Biruni, paper as a writing material was hardly known in India before the 11th century AD. Different accounts of travellers make it clear that paper came to be used in India in the late 11th century AD or early 12th century AD. The Commission has gone with option A, considering it to be early 12th century AD. In Bihar, Bihar Sharif and Arwal were important centres for the Paper Industry.

questions.question_text: Which type of land was called 'Aprahat'?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Aprahat was a land in which there was no cultivated forest land or cultivable wasteland, during the Gupta Period. Other types of land: Aprada (Unsettled land), Vastu (Habitat land), Khila (Untilled land).

questions.question_text: The author of 'Rajaniti Ratnakara' is

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Candesvara Thakura was a 14th-century political theorist from the Maithili-speaking region. He served as the Minister of War and Peace and the Chief Judge of the court under Harisimhadeva, the last King of the Karnata dynasty. His notable works include 'Rajaniti Ratnakara', which presents laws for state organization. K.P. Jayaswal recognised Rajanitiratnakara of Chandeshvara as one of the important compiled works of the 14th century, written in Sanskrit.

questions.question_text: Borobudur is the most wonderful Stupa in the world and its image and sculptures are the finest examples of

options.E: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanation: Borobudur, located in Magelang of Central Java, Indonesia, is said to be the greatest Buddhist stupa. A stupa is a mound that is filled with Buddhist relics. It is known for its decorations, statues, exceptional keeping, and conservation. It is one of the world's most complex buildings, built in Indo-Javanese Art.

questions.question_text: The account of the Moroccan traveller Ibn Batuta is known as

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Ibn Batuta was a Moroccan explorer and traveller known for his extensive travels, accounts of which were published in the book Rihla in Arabic. He came to India in 1334 during the reign of Muhammad Tughlaq and a part of the book is devoted to his experiences here. Ibn-Batutaha travelled extensively for 30 years and covered the territory equivalent to 44 modern countries.

questions.question_text: Which medieval Indian empire was famous for elaborating on 'local self-government'?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: The Chola empire was divided into Mandalams (Province), which were divided into Valanadu (Commissionary), Nadu (District), and Kurram (a group of villages). The arrangement of local self-government was the basic feature of the Chola administration. Cholas came to power after defeating Pallavas. The Chola Empire was founded by Vijayalaya. Rajendra Chola was the first to conquer the banks of the Ganga. The famous sculpture of Nataraja was the zenith of Cholas.

questions.question_text: Yousuf Shah Chak, the last Muslim ruler of Kashmir valley, who was exiled to Bihar by the Mughal emperor Akbar, was buried in

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Yousuf Shah Chak was a ruler of Kashmir, ruling from 1579 to 1586 AD. He was the last independent Muslim ruler of the Kashmir valley. Yousuf Shah Chak sought refuge in Sialkot before being brought to Delhi for talks with Akbar. Akbar's forces betrayed Yousuf and imprisoned him for the rest of his life. Yousuf was exiled to Bengal and later shifted to Bihar where he died. His grave remains in Bihar (the Tomb of Yousuf Shah lies in ruins at Nalanda).

questions.question_text: During which of the following King's reigns did the Persian traveller 'Abdur Razzak' come to India?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Dev Rai II was the greatest ruler of the Sangam dynasty and a great scholar of the Kannada and Sanskrit languages. Abdur Rajjak, a Persian chronicler, came to India during Dev Rai II's reign. In inscriptions, Dev Rai II took the title of 'Gajabetegara' meaning 'hunter of elephants'. He was also known as Praudh Deva Raya.

questions.question_text: Who was the founder of the Bahmani Kingdom?

options.E: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanation: Bahmani Kingdom (1347-1680) was founded by Allaudin Hasan Bahman Shah and his Guru 'Gangu'. Its capital was Gulbarga. Important rulers included Feroz Shah Bahman and Ahmed Shah Wali. Ahmad Shah Wali transferred the capital from Gulbarga to Bidar. Ibrahim Adil Shah introduced Dakhini in place of Persian as the court language. There was a constant tussle between the Vijayanagara Empire and the Bahmani Kingdom over Raichur Doab, Krishna-Godavari delta, and Marathawada.

questions.question_text: The Estate of Panchet is definitely mentioned as a part of Bihar in

options.E: None/more than one

questions.explanation: The Estate of Panchet is mentioned as a part of Bihar in Padshahnama. Presently, this area is in Jharkhand and there exists a famous Panchet Dam. Padshahnama was written by Abdul Hamid Lahori who was in the court of Shah Jahan.

questions.question_text: Banjaras during the medieval period of Indian history were generally
questions.explanation: The Banjaras were the most important trader-nomads during the medieval period. Their caravan was called Tanda. Sultan Alauddin Khalji used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets. Emperor Jahangir wrote in his memoirs that the Banjaras carried grain on their bullocks from different areas and sold it in towns. They also transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns.

questions.question_text: 'Kornish' was a form of?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: 'Uluq' & 'Dawa' in medieval times is related to?

options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: Mohammad bin Qasim, who was the first Muslim to have successfully invaded Sindh in 712 AD was commander of which Caliphate?

options.D: More than one of the above
options.E: None of the above

questions.question_text: The Apostle of Christ, St. Thomas who is credited with bringing Christianity to India, landed in which state of India 2000 years ago?

options.E: None of the above
